content.full_text:
Fern Polrrrcnl PRACTrcps CoN,rMrssroN
.128 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento. CA 9-58 1.1-1329
(916) 312--5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
September 9,2009
Kevin S. Alfaro
1344 Adams Street
St. Helena, California 94574
Re
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 4-09-190
Dear Mr. Alfaro:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the reporting provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1
QUESTION
Do you have a lO-percent or greater ownership interest in your accounting firm in
which there are nine partners and each partner has one vote?
CONCLUSION
Yes, you have a lO-percent or greater ownership interest in your accounting firm
in which there are nine partners and each partner has one vote because you have not
indicated that any partner has a greater interest in the firm than any other partner.
FACTS
You are a candidate for the St. Helena Unified School District Board of Trustees.
You are requesting advice regarding completion of your statement of economic interests
(Form 700). You would like advice as to whether you own a 1O-percent or greater
interest in a certified public accounting firm partnership because if so you would be
required to report this interest on your Statement of Economic Interests. Your firm
currently has nine partners and each partner has one vote. The partnership agreement
' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory ret'erences are to the GovernmentCode. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections lti I l0 through I 8997 of Title 2 of the Califbrnia
Code of Regulations. All regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Calitirrnia Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. I-09-190
Page No. 2
does not state ownership interests. Each partner receives a salary based on his or her core
responsibilities to the firm; this is similar to the salary of an employee but is technically
called a "guaranteed payment." Each partner has what is called a "capital account" with
the partnership. Retiring partners receive only the return of their individual capital
account. Your capital account represents about five percent of the total capital of the
firm. As of June 1,2007, any firm net profit after partner salaries are paid is distributed
in the following order: to pay off retiring partner buyout obligations, funding employees'
bonus pool, firm capital expenditures, interest on partner capital accounts, and partner
401(k) retirement accounts. If anything is left over, the executive/compensation
committee determines how to divide it based on subjective criteria. For the last two years
the firm has not had net profits. All partners are personally and severally guarantors of
the line of credit. The partnership agreement does not state ownership percentages. [f
the firm were to sell to a third party it is completely unclear in the partnership agreement
as to how to divide the sales proceeds in excess ofeach partner's capital account.
AIYALYSIS
One of the stated purposes of the Act is to ensure that public officials "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Section 81002(c) provides that the assets and income of public officials that may be
materially affected by their official actions should be disclosed. To implement these
provisions, the Act requires certain public officials to file statements of economic
interests disclosing their financial interests. (Sections 87 200, 87300, and 87 302.)
You are a candidate for the St. Helena Unified School District Board of Trustees.
As such, you are required under Section 87302(b) to file a statement of economic
interests (Form 700) disclosing your "investments, business positions, interest in real
property and income received . . . as enumerated in the disclosure requirements for that
position" (Form 700, Schedule C). Additionally, you are required to report investments,
income, and assets of business entities/trusts, in which you have an ownership interest of
lO-pe-rcent or greater, on Schedule A-2 of your statement of economic interests (Form
70q.2 You have indicated that your firm has nine partners all of which have equal
decision making power with one vote each. Your facts indicate that each partner
possesses equal ownership interest in the partnership, and that only the salaries, which are
based on work performed, differ. Therefore, based on the facts you have provided, you
have more than a l0-percent ownership interest (specifically, a one-ninth interest, or
approximately I 1.1 percent) in your accounting firm and this economic interest must be
reported on Schedule A-2 of your Form 700.
2 Regulation 18703.1(b) provides that a public official has an economic interest in a business
entity if . . . "[tJhe public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee. employee, or holds any position of
management in the business entity."


File No. t-09-190
Page No. 3
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
4/2r.y'.,6aa
By: Sukhi K. Brar
Counsel, Legal Division
SKB jgl

sections.question:
Do you have a lO-percent or greater ownership interest in your accounting firm in
which there are nine partners and each partner has one vote?
sections.conclusion:
Yes, you have a lO-percent or greater ownership interest in your accounting firm
in which there are nine partners and each partner has one vote because you have not
indicated that any partner has a greater interest in the firm than any other partner.

interest in a certified public accounting firm partnership because if so you would be

the firm were to sell to a third party it is completely unclear in the partnership agreement
as to how to divide the sales proceeds in excess ofeach partner's capital account.

approximately I 1.1 percent) in your accounting firm and this economic interest must be
reported on Schedule A-2 of your Form 700.
2 Regulation 18703.1(b) provides that a public official has an economic interest in a business
entity if . . . "[tJhe public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee. employee, or holds any position of
management in the business entity."


File No. t-09-190
